title: Split Checkout Into Order Services
context: The Holiday Checkout Freeze incident showed that cart validation, inventory checks, payment confirmation, order creation, and delivery scheduling were too tightly coupled.
discussion_summary: The team agreed that the checkout system was easy to understand but too fragile during traffic spikes. They chose to split checkout responsibilities so each part could scale and fail more safely.

alternatives_considered.option: Add more servers to the existing checkout system
Split checkout into cart, order, inventory reservation, and delivery scheduling services

final_decision: Create CheckoutFlow by splitting the checkout monolith into cart validation, order creation, inventory reservation, and delivery scheduling services.
reasons: Checkout froze because too many steps happened in one synchronous path.
Holiday traffic needed independent scaling.
Support needed clearer ownership when an order got stuck.

title: Use Order Lifecycle Events
context: After splitting checkout, FreshCart needed a clean way to tell inventory, delivery, search, and support systems when an order changed state.
discussion_summary: The team chose order lifecycle events so downstream systems could react without blocking checkout. Lucas emphasized retries, monitoring, and event lag visibility.

alternatives_considered.option: Keep direct synchronous calls between systems

final_decision: Publish order lifecycle events for order placed, inventory reserved, shopper picking, packed, out for delivery, delivered, delayed, and cancelled.
reasons: Checkout should not wait for every downstream system.
DeliveryTrack needed near-real-time customer status.
StockSync needed reliable order updates to adjust inventory.

title: Add Inventory Reservation Service
context: Customers were ordering items that looked available online but were already gone from store shelves.
discussion_summary: Priya proposed reserving inventory during checkout and releasing it if payment failed or the order was cancelled. Product wanted the behavior explained clearly to customers.

alternatives_considered.option: Reserve items during checkout with automatic release rules

final_decision: Build StockSync inventory reservations so items are temporarily held during checkout and released if the order does not complete.
reasons: Out-of-stock surprises damaged customer trust.
Reservations reduce double-selling the last item.
Support needed a clear explanation for substitutions and unavailable items.

title: Create Customer-Friendly Delivery Statuses
context: Customers and support agents could see internal order flags but did not understand whether an order was being picked, packed, delayed, or out for delivery.
discussion_summary: The team decided to translate internal events into simple delivery statuses and keep driver/customer-sensitive details hidden from broad support access.
final_decision: Build DeliveryTrack around customer-friendly statuses: order placed, shopper picking, packed, out for delivery, delayed, delivered, and cancelled.
reasons: Customers needed plain-language order status.
Support agents needed the same view customers saw.
Raw internal states exposed too much implementation detail.

title: Deduplicate Delivery Notifications
context: Some customers received the same out-for-delivery message multiple times after event retries.
discussion_summary: The group agreed that retries are normal, so every customer notification event needed a stable idempotency key.

alternatives_considered.option: Deduplicate only in the mobile app
Add idempotency keys to notification events

final_decision: Add idempotency keys to delivery and order notification events so repeated messages are ignored safely.
reasons: Retries can send the same event more than once.
Customers lose trust when delivery messages repeat.
Support needed confidence that duplicate messages did not mean duplicate orders.

title: Track Inventory Freshness
context: Inventory updates from stores sometimes lagged behind customer search and checkout screens.
discussion_summary: The team decided to display and monitor freshness signals so stale inventory could be detected before it caused bad orders.

alternatives_considered.option: Manually review stale stores each morning
Track freshness timestamps and alert on stale inventory

final_decision: Add freshness timestamps and stale-inventory alerts to StockSync.
reasons: Search and checkout needed to know when stock data was old.
Store feeds could fail silently.
Support needed evidence when substitutions increased.

title: Limit Support Access To Customer Data
context: Support agents needed delivery context, but they did not need full customer address history or driver location traces.
discussion_summary: Sofia pushed for least-privilege support views while Amara defined what support actually needed to answer customers.

alternatives_considered.option: Escalate every delivery question to engineering
Create a curated support context panel

final_decision: Create a support context panel with safe order status, delay reason, latest customer-visible message, and escalation links.
reasons: Support needed faster answers.
Customer and driver data needed privacy boundaries.
A curated panel reduced engineering interruptions.

title: Order Event Schema Governance
context: CheckoutFlow, StockSync, DeliveryTrack, support dashboards, and analytics all depended on the same order events.
discussion_summary: The team agreed that changing event fields casually could break inventory, delivery status, and customer notifications.

alternatives_considered.option: Let each producer change events independently

final_decision: Create versioned order event contracts with compatibility checks, idempotency fields, and privacy review for sensitive attributes.
reasons: Many systems depended on order events.
Schema changes could break customer-facing status.
Sensitive customer and driver fields needed review.

title: Create FreshCart Decision Memory
context: New teammates repeatedly asked why checkout was split, why inventory reservations existed, and who understood delivery notification problems.
discussion_summary: The team proposed EchoMind as a memory layer that connects incidents, architecture decisions, tickets, PRs, and Slack discussions.

alternatives_considered.option: Use Jira as the only decision record

final_decision: Build EchoMind to connect FreshCart decisions, incidents, Jira tickets, pull requests, and discussions.
reasons: Teams needed to understand why systems changed.
Incident follow-ups were hard to connect to implementation.
Expert finding mattered during support escalations.

title: Use EchoMind For Onboarding
context: FreshCart wanted new engineers and support leads to understand the order system story without reading dozens of old tickets.
discussion_summary: The team agreed that EchoMind should start with a guided company story and then let users drill into evidence.

alternatives_considered.option: Send new hires a long document
Use EchoMind guided memory plus source evidence

final_decision: Use EchoMind as an onboarding and decision-history tool with guided story, experts, timeline, and citations.
reasons: The story needed to be understandable before showing raw artifacts.
Evidence still needed to be available for trust.
Support and engineering needed the same shared memory.
